plan.thesis: Prompt caching lets you pay once to store a large context and reuse it at a fraction of the cost — but only when applied to the right workloads.
plan.targetTakeaway: Readers will leave knowing exactly which of their LLM calls are cacheable, how to configure caching in the Anthropic API, and what 60–90% cost reductions look like in practice.

sections.title: Why Your LLM Costs Are Higher Than They Need to Be
sections.intent: Hook the reader with a concrete cost-pain scenario; introduce caching as the solution.
keyPoints: A typical RAG call re-sends the same 10k-token system prompt every time
That's ~$0.03 per call multiplied by millions of requests
Prompt caching changes the economics fundamentally

sections.title: How Prompt Caching Works: Tokens, TTL, and Cache Keys
sections.intent: Explain the technical mechanism: what gets cached, for how long, and how the provider identifies cache hits.
keyPoints: Cache is keyed on the exact prefix of the messages array
Anthropic's TTL is 5 minutes (extendable via usage)
Input tokens on a cache hit cost ~10% of full price
cache_control breakpoint markers in the API

profile.name: Habr
profile.audience: Russian-speaking backend engineers with 3–10 years of experience

sectionDrafts.contentMd:
## Why Your LLM Costs Are Higher Than They Need to Be

You've built a RAG pipeline. Every query sends a 10 000-token system prompt to the model before a single word of user content. At $0.003 per 1K input tokens that's $0.03 per call. At a million calls a month you're paying $30 000 just to re-transmit context the model has already seen a million times.

Prompt caching changes this calculus. Cache a prefix once, and every subsequent call that shares that prefix pays only 10% of the normal input price for those tokens.

## How Prompt Caching Works: Tokens, TTL, and Cache Keys

Prompt caching works by storing the KV (key-value) computation for a prefix of your messages array on Anthropic's servers. The cache is keyed on the exact byte sequence of that prefix — any change invalidates it. By default the TTL is 5 minutes, extended each time the entry is read.

To mark a breakpoint you add `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}` to the last message in the prefix you want cached. The API response then includes `cache_creation_input_tokens` and `cache_read_input_tokens` counters so you can track hit rate.

snapshot.heroBrief: An overhead editorial photo of an open laptop on a wooden desk, a stylized translucent overlay showing duplicated lines of code being collapsed into a single cached block, conveying the idea of paying once and reusing many times.

inlineSlots.brief: A clean isometric illustration of a long pipeline with one stage repeatedly re-sending the same large brick of context to an LLM, while a meter on the side ticks up dollars — emphasizing wasted re-transmission.
A diagrammatic explainer image: a prefix of message bubbles glowing as 'cached', followed by a fresh user-message bubble; a tiny 5-minute clock floats above the cached bubbles to convey TTL.
